This letter is SUPERSEDED by the 1998 amendments to
Regulation f 8530. Regulation 18530 revised the Commission's
interpretation of Government Code Section 85300. Prior to the
amendment, the Commission interpreted Section 85300 to prohibit the
use of public resources for campaign purposes. Amended Regulation
18530 provides that Section 85300 only prohibits the payment of public
moneys for the public financing of elections.


California
Fair P01itical
Practices Commission
February 27‚Ä≤  ‚ñÝ990
Eva Gatling
Public Infornation officer
city of carson
701 East Carson Street
P.O. Box 6234
Carson, CA 9O7 49
Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our FiIe No. I-9 0-04 8
Dear !ts. GatI ing:
This is in response to your Ietter requesting assistance on
behalf of Carson City Officials regarding the Proposition 73
amendments to the Political Reforn Act (the trActx).r/ 
Please
note, the Act requires the commission to provide formal written
advice only to persons whose duties under the Act are in question
or their authorized representative. The Commission iri1l not
provide formal written advice to a representative unless the name
of the person whose duties are in question is provided. (section
83114 (b) ; Regulation 18329(b) (2) (A) , copy enclosed. )
consequently, we can only provide the folJowing infornal
guidelines with respect to your question.z/
Further, the Commission cannot deternine \rhether the
expenditures for a television prograrn approved by the Carson City
Council are legitj-mate governDent expenditures. (See, e.q. Pena1
Sperl (1976) 54 Ca1. App. 3d 540; and Peop1e v. Battin ($7e) 77
cal. App. 3d 635.) We can only advise as to whether such
expenditures are a violation of the provisions of the political
Reforrn Act. Other provisions of the law which nay apply to these
facts, such as Penal code Section 424 which deals with the misuse
L/ Government Code Sections B1OOO-91015. A]I statutory refer-
ences are to the Goverru[ent code unless otherwise indicated. Con-
mission regulations appear at 2 California code of Regrulations
Section 18000, et seo. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2/ rnformal assistance does not
imrnunity provided by an opinion
83114; Regulation 18329 (c) (3) . )
SUPERSEDED BY 1998
AMENDMENTS To
REGULAT10N 18530
provide the requestor with the
or fornal written advice. (Section

95804„Éª0807 0 (916)322‚Äê5660


File No. I-9 0-04 8
Page 2
of public moneys, are outside the jurisdiction of the Commis-
sion.r/ 
Thus, nothing in this letter should be construed to be an
authorization for any specific expenditures made by a state or
Iocal government agency.
OUESTTON
Is the use of public funds by the City of Carson to pay for a
television progran hosted by elected city officials a violation of
the Act as amended by Proposition 73?
CONCLUSION
Distribution of a progran over the airwaves is not the
distribution of a tangible iten. 
Consequently, televised programs
are not prohibited mass mailings under the Act provided they are
not delivered as tangible itens to the recipient,s horne, post of-
fice box or office.
Moreover, the use of public funds in the production of a
television prograD hosted by city officials 
does not violate the
prohibition in Section 85300, provided the progran is not used to
advocate or promote any elected official,s 
election to office.
However, this advice app).ies only to the provisions of the Act.
Other restrictions may be inposed by laws that are not set forth
in the Act and are, therefore, outside the Cornrnission,s jurisdic-
tion.
FACTS
The city of carson is contemplating the productj,on of a
tel-evision program to publicize city issues. The program wiIl be
hosted by various elected city officials 
and staff and paid for
with public funds. You have recently becone concerned that use of
public funds to produce the television program nay be a misuse of
public money in violation of the Proposition 73 amendments to the
Act.
ANALYSI S
Mass Mailinqs
In June 1988 Proposition 23 amended Section 89001 of the Act.
This section now provides: rtNo newsletter or other mass nailing
shall be sent at public expense.rr However, a literal reading oi
this section 1ed to a conclusion that all mass nailings invoiving
public funds, irrespective of content or purpose, wer6 prohibited.
In response to a variety of questions concerning the diatribution
of tax notj.ces, tax refund checks, conmunity college schedules,
sanple baIlots, and other uass mailings custonarily sent by
Jt 
Penal code Section 424
district 
attorney and the
is interpreted and enforced by the
Attorney ceneral.


File No. I-9 o-o4 a
Page 3
government agencies, the Conmission adopted Regulation 189011/ to
clarify hrhich nailings were pemissible and which were prohibited
under the Act. 5 /
Regulation 18901(a) provides that a mass rnailing is
prohibited only if all the follos/ing apply:
(1) Any iten sent is delivered, by any
neans, to the recipient at his or her
residence, place of ernploynent or business, or
post office box. For purposes of subdivision
(a) (1), the iten delivered to the recipient
must be a tanqible item, such as a videotape,
record. or button, or a r/ri,tten docunent.
(2) The iten sent either3
(A) Features an elected officer
affiliated with the agency which produces
or sends the mailing, or
(B) Includes the name, office,
photograph, or other reference to an
elected officer affiliated with the
agency which produces or sends the mail-
ing, and is prepared or sent in co-
operation, consultation, coordination, or
concert $rith the elected officer;
(3) (A) Any of the costs of distribution
is paid for with public Doneys,' or
(B) Costs of design, production,
and printing exceeding $50.00 are paid
with public noneys, and the design,
production, or printing is done with the
intent of sending the iten other than as
pernitted by this regrulation.
4/ Regulation 18901 rras amended. by the Connissj.on in December
1989. The regulation will be subrnitted to the Office of
Adninistrative Law in the near future. Effective January 18,
1990, the Corumission began advising on the basis of the language
of the new regulation. The issues in this letter have been
analyzed under the aBended reg'ulation. A copy of the anended
regulation is enclosed.
5/ The Comnission,s authority to interpret Section g9oo1 to avoid
the absurd results of its literal application was upheld in watson
v. Fair Political Practices Com., Case No. 8042250, Second Dist.
Court of Appeal, 90 D.A.R. 1312 (February 6, 1990).


File No. I-90-048
Page 4
(4) More than tsro hundred substantially
sinilar items are sent, in a single calendar
rnonth, excluding any itetn sent in response to
an unsolicited request and any itero described
in subdivision (b);
(Bnphasis added. )
According to Regnrlation 18901, a publicly-funded nailing is a
prohibited nass nailing if it is delivered as a tangible iten to
the recipient at his or her residence, place of enploynent or
business, or post office box. Since distribution over the
airwaves is not the distribution of a tangible item, televised
programs are not prohibited ruass mailings under the Act.
Exrcenditures for the Purpose of Seeking ELective Office
Section 85300 also sets forth a restriction on the use of
public funds, section 85300 provides:
No public officer shall expend and no
candidate sha1l accept any public moneys for the
purpose of seeking elective office.
Thus, section 85300 prohibits the use of public noneys6/ for
the purpose of seeking elective office, 
However, s/hen
expenditures are for the purpose_gf seeking elective office was
not specified in Proposition 73.'/ 
uoreover, prior to the enact-
nent of Proposition 73, the Coromission was never confronted
directly uith the issue of when paynents are for trthe purpose of
seeking elective of f ice. rr
The Commission has, however, dealt with issues concerning
disclosure of campaign expenditures. Prior to the enactment of
Proposition 73, the Commission adopted Regulation 18420 (copy
enclosed), which addresses the question of rrhether palments by a
government agency uight be canpaign expenditures. If a paynent is
determj,ned to be a canpaign expenditure, the Act requires the
6/ 
t'Public moneysrr is defined to include all bonds and evidence
of indebtedness, and all noneys belonging to the state, or any
city, county, to$rn, district, 
or public agency therej-n, and atl
moneys, bonds, and evidence of indebtedness received or held by
state, county, district, 
city, town, or public agency officers in
their official capacity. (section 85102(e); penal code Section
426.t
'/ - The language in. the ballot arguments regarding proposition 73
and Proposition 68 indicate that the Section 85300 of Droposition
73 was a direct rebuttal to the pubtic financing aspects if
Proposition 68. There was no indication of the apptication of the
provision outside of the public financing context.-


File No. I-90-04I
Page 5
disclosure of the payDent by the
the Iocal government agency that
1842o provides:
beneficiary of the pa)rment or by
made the palrment. Regulation
For purposes of this subsection, "carnpaign
activitiestt shall include, but are not liroited to,
the foI lowing:
(1) Arranging or coordinating a
campaign-related event;
(2') Acting in the capacity of the
campaign manager or coordinator;
(3) Soliciting, receivj-ng or
acknowledging campaign contributions or ar-
ranging for the raising of contributions;
(4) Developing, writing or distributing
canpaign literature or naking arrangements for
campaign Iiterature,'
(5) Arranging for the development,
production or distribution of campaj,gn
Iiterature;
(6) Preparing television, radio or
newspaper campaign advertisements;
(7) Arranging for the development,
production, publishing or broadcast of
caropaign advertisements;
(8) Establishing liaison with or co-
ordinating activities of campaign volunteers I
(9) Preparing canpaign budgets;
(10) Preparing canpaign statements; and
(11) Participating in partisan get out
the vote drives.
In light of this
85300 was intended to
Regulation 
‚ñÝ8420(b)„ÄÇ
authority‚Ä≤  we conclude that Section
the pub‚ñÝic financ‚ñÝng of e‚ñÝection
that Section 85300 was intended to
existing
prohibit
believe
campaigns. We do not
prohib j,t everv public
benefit an el,ected of f j-ciaI,s chances for reelection. Arguably,
aLl the activities of incumbent elected officers in the
performance of their governnental duties indirectly benefit their
reelection. Instead, we conclude that Section 85300 was intended
to prohibit the public financing of election campaigns.


File No. I-9 0-O4I
Page 6
Consistent lrith this intent, where public moneys are spent to
advocate or promote a candidate,s election to public office, Sec-
tion 853OO has been violated. 
We believe it is appropriate to
consider rrcampaign activities" as defined in Regulation 18420 as
being among the types of activities for which Section 85300
prohibits the expenditure of public moneys. This approach is
consistent with the approach taken by the courts concerning use of
public funds in election campaigns. The courts have distinguished
bet\.reen the appropriate use of public funds to give the pubic a
balanced infornational presentation of issues surrounding an elec-
tion campaign, and the unlawful use of public funds by a govern-
tnent agency to take sides in an election campaign. (Stanson v.
llott, supra; Leaque of women Voters v. Countvwide crim. Justice
Coordination Corn. (1988) 203 CaI. App. 3d 529.)
Thus, the Act does not prohibit the use of public funds to
pay for a televj.sion prograrn hosted by elected city official.s,
provided the progran is not produced for the purpose of furthering
the elected officiaLs' election to office. 
l{here in fact an
elected official 
uses the progran to advocate his or her reelec-
tion or candidacy, the expenditure of public funds is for the
purpose of seeking elective office and would be in violation of
section 85300.
If you have any further questions regarding this natter,
please feel free to contact ne at (916) 322-59OL.
Sincerely,
Kathryn E. Donovan
l counsel

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Is the use of public funds by the City of Carson to pay for a
television progran hosted by elected city officials a violation of
the Act as amended by Proposition 73?

CONCLUSION: Distribution of a progran over the airwaves is not the
distribution of a tangible iten. 
Consequently, televised programs
are not prohibited mass mailings under the Act provided they are
not delivered as tangible itens to the recipient,s horne, post of-
fice box or office.
Moreover, the use of public funds in the production of a
television prograD hosted by city officials 
does not violate the
prohibition in Section 85300, provided the progran is not used to
advocate or promote any elected official,s 
election to office.
However, this advice app).ies only to the provisions of the Act.
Other restrictions may be inposed by laws that are not set forth
in the Act and are, therefore, outside the Cornrnission,s jurisdic-
tion.

Prior to the amendment, the Commission interpreted Section 85300 to prohibit the use of public resources for campaign purposes. Amended Regulation 18530 provides that Section 85300 only prohibits the payment of public moneys for the public financing of elections.
